docs.title: Welcome to Getting Started Contributing to Apache Airflow: Quickstart Onboarding Workshop
docs.text:
What we'll learn in this workshop:

  1. Preparing the Local Development Environment
  2. GitHub Setup
  3. Installing Breeze
  4. Starting Contributing
  5. After Your PR Is Merged (or Before Your Next Contribution)

docs.title: After Your PR Is Merged (or Before Your Next Contribution)
docs.text: Keeping your fork up to date is important before starting a new branch.

docs.title: Sync with Upstream
docs.text:
To fetch, rebase, and push:

git switch main
git fetch upstream
git rebase upstream/main
git push origin main

Your fork will be aligned with the official repository. Now you're ready to create a new branch and start your next contribution. 🚀

docs.title: Add the upstream remote and verify
docs.text:
git remote add upstream git@github.com:apache/airflow.git
git remote -v

You should see both:

  • origin → your fork
  • upstream → apache/airflow

docs.title: Install Docker
docs.text: Ensure Docker is installed and running on your machine.

docs.title: Start Contributing
docs.text:
In this workshop, we'll start with documentation or translation contributions. These are the easiest and most beginner-friendly ways to get involved.

You don't need to understand Airflow internals — just improve clarity or language support.

docs.title: Contributing to Documentation
docs.text:
Edit a documentation file, e.g., airflow-ctl/docs/index.rst.

Make a small improvement:

  • Fix a typo
  • Improve a sentence
  • Clarify wording
  • Update formatting

Keep the change small and focused.

Build the documentation.

breeze build-docs apache-airflow-ctl

Preview the documentation locally by running ./docs/start_doc_server.sh for a lighter resource option.

Then open:

  • http://localhost:8000
  • Documentation list http://localhost:8000/docs/

Verify your change appears correctly.

docs.title: Contributing to Translation (UI i18n)
docs.text:
Another easy entry point is improving UI translations.

Read the Internationalization (i18n) policy here. This explains how translations are structured.

To check translation completeness:

breeze ui check-translation-completeness

To check a specific language (e.g., Thai):

breeze ui check-translation-completeness --language th

You can:

  • Improve existing translations
  • Add missing translations
  • Correct unclear wording
